original_abstract:
We describe redMaPPer, a new red-sequence cluster finder specifically
designed to make optimal use of ongoing and near-future large photometric
surveys. The algorithm has multiple attractive features: (1) It can iteratively
self-train the red-sequence model based on minimal spectroscopic training
sample, an important feature for high redshift surveys; (2) It can handle
complex masks with varying depth; (3) It produces cluster-appropriate random
points to enable large-scale structure studies; (4) All clusters are assigned a
full redshift probability distribution P(z); (5) Similarly, clusters can have
multiple candidate central galaxies, each with corresponding centering
probabilities; (6) The algorithm is parallel and numerically efficient: it can
run a Dark Energy Survey-like catalog in ~500 CPU hours; (7) The algorithm
exhibits excellent photometric redshift performance, the richness estimates are
tightly correlated with external mass proxies, and the completeness and purity
of the corresponding catalogs is superb. We apply the redMaPPer algorithm to
~10,000 deg^2 of SDSS DR8 data, and present the resulting catalog of ~25,000
clusters over the redshift range 0.08<z<0.55. The redMaPPer photometric
redshifts are nearly Gaussian, with a scatter \sigma_z ~ 0.006 at z~0.1,
increasing to \sigma_z~0.02 at z~0.5 due to increased photometric noise near
the survey limit. The median value for |\Delta z|/(1+z) for the full sample is
0.006. The incidence of projection effects is low (<=5%). Detailed performance
comparisons of the redMaPPer DR8 cluster catalog to X-ray and SZ catalogs are
presented in a companion paper (Rozo & Rykoff 2014).
humanized_abstract: We introduce redMaPPer, a new red-sequence cluster finder designed to make efficient use of current and future survey data. This version of the algorithm has a number of highly desirable features: - Self-training to (optionally) create a robust red-sequence model given a small training set, an issue for large surveys at high redshift) - Handling of complex masks with varying depth - Capability to create large numbers of random points useful for large scale structure studies - Full posterior distribution of redshift for each cluster found - Multiple galaxies are identified as potential centers for each cluster with associated centering posterior distributions - Highly parallel and efficient, (running a DES-like catalog down to 24 takes approximately 500 CPU hours) - Good photoz, tight correlation between measured richness and true mass, and extremely high completeness and purity. We utilize 10,000 deg^2 of SDSS DR8, producing a sample of 25k high purity clusters with redshifts between 0.08-0.55. The photo-z distributions of the redMaPPer clusters are nearly Gaussian, with an intrinsic scatter of \sigma_z\approx0.006 at z=0.1 increasing out to \sigma_z\approx0.02 at z=0.5. The median value for |\\Delta z|/(1+z) for the full sample is 0.006. Also, the impact from projected clusters is shown to be less than 5%. For a detailed comparison with X-ray and SZ cluster samples using redMaPPer DR8, see Rozo & Rykoff (2014).

We utilize 10,000 deg^2 of SDSS DR8, producing a sample of 25k high purity clusters with redshifts between 0.08-0.55. The photo-z distributions of the redMaPPer clusters are nearly Gaussian, with an intrinsic scatter of \sigma_z\approx0.006 at z=0.1 increasing out to \sigma_z\approx0.02 at z=0.5.
